question: A 54-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.9%) has no visual symptoms. He last had an eye exam 4 years ago. Fundoscopic exam in clinic is limited. Which screening approach is most appropriate?
options: A) No eye screening needed until the patient reports blurred vision
B) Refer for dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually thereafter because diabetic retinopathy can be asymptomatic and vision-threatening
C) Visual acuity check in primary care alone is sufficient indefinitely
D) Screening only every 5 years if HbA1c remains below 10%
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Refer for dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually thereafter because diabetic retinopathy can be asymptomatic and vision-threatening).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Diabetic retinopathy (DR) progresses silently until advanced—microaneurysms, hemorrhages, macular edema, and neovascularization cause irreversible vision loss if untreated. Patients may retain normal acuity until macular involvement or vitreous hemorrhage. Duration of diabetes, poor glycemic control, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and renal disease increase risk.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Chronic hyperglycemia damages retinal capillaries via advanced glycation end-products, oxidative stress, and inflammation—leading to microaneurysms, capillary non-perfusion, and VEGF-driven neovascularization in proliferative DR. Macular edema is the leading cause of vision loss in nonproliferative DR.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
ADA standards: initial dilated retinal exam at diabetes diagnosis (type 2) or within 5 years of type 1 onset, then at least annually; every 2 years if one or more normal exams and well-controlled. Nonmydriatic retinal photography in validated programs may substitute if interpreted by trained readers with timely referral for unreadable or abnormal images. Primary care fundoscopy has limited sensitivity—formal ophthalmic exam preferred.

MANAGEMENT:
Optimize glycemic control (DCCT/UKPDS: tight control reduces DR progression), blood pressure (ACEi/ARB), and lipid management. Refer abnormal screens urgently: macular edema and proliferative DR need intravitreal anti-VEGF, laser photocoagulation, or surgery. Pregnancy in diabetes requires exam each trimester. Document screening completion—gap in care here (4 years) warrants immediate referral.

KEY POINTS:
Diabetic retinopathy screening: dilated eye exam at least annually—not wait for symptoms or acuity alone. Poorly controlled type 2 DM with overdue exam needs urgent referral. Vision symptoms indicate possible advanced disease.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Dilated comprehensive eye exam now and at least annually).
- Diabetic retinopathy is often asymptomatic until advanced; annual dilated exam is standard of care.
- Type 2 diabetes requires screening at diagnosis and regularly thereafter regardless of symptoms.
- Visual acuity alone or 5-year intervals with high HbA1c miss treatable retinopathy.

question: A 65-year-old woman reports sudden painless monocular vision loss lasting 90 seconds while reading, with full recovery. She has atrial fibrillation on apixaban and carotid bruit on exam. Which evaluation is most appropriate?
options: A) Reassure because vision returned; no workup needed
B) Treat as amaurosis fugax/transient monocular vision loss—urgent evaluation for embolic source (carotid imaging, cardiac workup) and stroke prevention optimization despite anticoagulation
C) Refer only to optometry for refractive error
D) Start topical antibiotics for conjunctivitis
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Treat as amaurosis fugax/transient monocular vision loss—urgent evaluation for embolic source [carotid imaging, cardiac workup] and stroke prevention optimization despite anticoagulation).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Amaurosis fugax is transient monocular vision loss often described as a curtain descending over one eye, lasting minutes, representing retinal ischemia—analogous to TIA. Complete recovery does not eliminate stroke risk. Associated carotid bruit and atrial fibrillation increase embolic probability. Persistent vision loss suggests central retinal artery occlusion (stroke equivalent requiring emergent evaluation).

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Emboli (carotid atherosclerotic plaque, cardioembolic from AF) or hemodynamic hypoperfusion occlude central/branch retinal artery temporarily. Ipsilateral carotid stenosis ≥70% is a major source. Even on anticoagulation, patients may have additional atherosclerotic or hypoperfusion mechanisms.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Urgent same-day evaluation: detailed history (duration, pattern), BP, carotid auscultation, fundoscopy (Hollenhorst plaque), ECG for AF, consider echocardiography. Carotid duplex ultrasound assesses stenosis; CTA/MRA if indicated. Brain MRI may identify concurrent silent embolic strokes. Differentiate from migraine visual aura (positive scintillations, bilateral or homonymous field defects, headache), giant cell arteritis (headache, jaw claudication in older patients), and acute angle-closure glaucoma (painful red eye).

MANAGEMENT:
Stroke prevention pathway: optimize anticoagulation adherence for AF; manage vascular risk factors (BP, lipids, smoking). Symptomatic high-grade carotid stenosis may warrant endarterectomy/stenting per neurology/vascular surgery. Low-dose aspirin may be added in selected cases per specialist guidance when anticoagulation alone and embolic source persists. Educate on stroke warning signs and emergent care for prolonged vision loss.

KEY POINTS:
Transient monocular vision loss = retinal TIA—urgent embolic workup despite symptom resolution. Do not reassure without evaluation. AF plus carotid bruit raises embolic risk; anticoagulation does not obviate vascular imaging.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Urgent embolic workup and stroke prevention for amaurosis fugax).
- Sudden painless monocular vision loss with recovery suggests retinal TIA (amaurosis fugax).
- Requires carotid and cardiac evaluation despite full recovery and existing anticoagulation.
- Reassurance without workup, optometry alone, or antibiotics miss a stroke warning sign.

question: A 6-year-old boy has 2 days of otalgia, fever 38.5°C, and irritability. Otoscopy shows bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with obscured landmarks and limited mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. He has no mastoid tenderness or neurologic signs. Which treatment is most appropriate?
options: A) Oral amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID for 5–10 days (or amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high local failure rates)
B) Topical antibiotic drops only without examining tympanic membrane status
C) Oral azithromycin as universal first-line regardless of allergy history
D) Observation only in all children under 12 regardless of severity
explanation:
CORRECT: A (Oral amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID for 5–10 days [or amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high local failure rates]).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acute otitis media (AOM) presents with otalgia, fever, irritability in young children, and bulging erythematous TM with poor mobility on pneumatic otoscopy—distinguishing from otitis media with effusion (retracted TM, no acute inflammation). Severe illness (moderate-severe otalgia, fever ≥39°C), bilateral disease in children <2, or otorrhea with AOM warrant antibiotic therapy per AAP guidelines.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Eustachian tube dysfunction after viral URI allows bacterial ascent (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis). Bulging TM reflects middle-ear pressure and inflammation. Complications include mastoiditis, perforation, and rarely intracranial extension if untreated in severe cases.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Diagnosis requires acute onset and middle-ear effusion (bulging TM, impaired mobility, air-fluid level, perforation with purulent otorrhea) plus inflammation. Distinguish external otitis (tragal tenderness, canal edema). Red TM alone from crying/fever insufficient. Assess for mastoid tenderness, meningismus, facial nerve palsy (urgent).

MANAGEMENT:
Antibiotics for children meeting severe criteria or age <2 with bilateral AOM; shared decision for unilateral nonsevere in older children. First-line amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day (high-dose targets penicillin-resistant pneumococcus); amoxicillin-clavulanate if amoxicillin within 30 days or treatment failure. Duration 5–10 days per severity/age. Analgesia (acetaminophen/ibuprofen) essential. Topical drops alone treat otitis externa, not intact TM AOM. Follow-up if no improvement in 48–72 hours.

KEY POINTS:
Bulging TM with fever in child = AOM requiring antibiotics per severity/age guidelines—not observation for severe case or topical drops alone. High-dose amoxicillin first-line; azithromycin only for penicillin allergy.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: A (High-dose amoxicillin for acute otitis media with bulging TM and fever).
- Bulging erythematous immobile tympanic membrane with fever confirms AOM in a child.
- First-line: amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day; amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent amoxicillin or high failure rates.
- Topical drops alone, universal azithromycin, or observation in severe young child are incorrect.

question: A 5-year-old unvaccinated child presents with high fever, drooling, muffled voice, and stridor at rest in tripod position. He appears toxic. Oxygen saturation is 92% on room air. Which immediate action is most appropriate?
options: A) Aggressive oropharyngeal examination with tongue depressor in clinic to confirm diagnosis

B) Do not agitate or examine throat; keep child calm, administer supplemental oxygen, call emergency services for airway management and hospital transfer—suspect epiglottitis or severe supraglottic obstruction
C) Prescribe oral amoxicillin and send home
D) Nebulized albuterol alone for presumed asthma
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Do not agitate or examine throat; keep child calm, administer supplemental oxygen, call emergency services for airway management and hospital transfer—suspect epiglottitis or severe supraglottic obstruction).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Acute epiglottitis (now rare post-Hib vaccine) presents with rapid onset high fever, severe sore throat, drooling (inability to swallow), muffled “hot potato” voice, stridor, and toxic appearance—classically leaning forward (tripod). Unlike croup, minimal cough and more abrupt deterioration. Bacterial supraglottitis can progress to complete airway obstruction within hours.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
Haemophilus influenzae type b historically; now Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and other organisms in unvaccinated or immunocompromised. Supraglottic edema obstructs airway; manipulation may trigger laryngospasm and arrest.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Clinical diagnosis in unstable patient—do NOT perform tongue blade examination in office (may precipitate obstruction). Lateral neck X-ray may show thumbprint sign if child stable in controlled setting. Direct visualization reserved for operating room with anesthesia/ENT prepared for intubation. Distinguish from croup (barking cough, gradual URI prodrome, steeple sign) and retropharyngeal abscess (neck stiffness, limited extension).

MANAGEMENT:
Airway first: allow child to remain in preferred position, minimize procedures, supplemental oxygen, emergent EMS/hospital transfer to facility with pediatric airway capability. IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) after airway secured. Corticosteroids and racemic epinephrine may be used per protocol in controlled setting. Intubation/tracheostomy if impending obstruction. Unvaccinated status warrants Hib consideration and public health notification.

Primary care must recognize and not attempt office throat exam.

KEY POINTS:
Toxic child with drooling, stridor, tripod posture: epiglottitis until proven otherwise—no throat exam in office; emergent airway management. Oral antibiotics outpatient or albuterol alone are dangerous.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Do not examine throat; oxygen, EMS, controlled airway management).
- Drooling, muffled voice, stridor at rest, and tripod posture suggest epiglottitis/supraglottic obstruction.
- Avoid agitating or examining oropharynx in clinic; emergent hospital transfer for airway control.
- Office throat exam, outpatient amoxicillin, or albuterol alone risk fatal obstruction.

question: A 32-year-old contact lens wearer presents with acute red eye, foreign body sensation, photophobia, and watery discharge. Visual acuity is slightly reduced. Slit-lamp exam shows corneal fluorescein uptake in a dendritic pattern. Which management is most appropriate?
options: A) Topical corticosteroid drops to reduce inflammation
B) Oral or topical antiviral therapy (e.g., oral valacyclovir) and ophthalmology referral; discontinue contact lenses; avoid steroids which can worsen HSV keratitis
C) Topical antibiotic drops only for bacterial conjunctivitis
D) Continue contact lens wear with lubricating drops
explanation:
CORRECT: B (Oral or topical antiviral therapy [e.g., oral valacyclovir] and ophthalmology referral; discontinue contact lenses; avoid steroids which can worsen HSV keratitis).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS:
Herpes simplex keratitis presents with unilateral red eye, pain, photophobia, blurred vision, and dendritic corneal ulcer on fluorescein staining—branching epithelial defects with terminal bulbs pathognomonic for HSV. Contact lens wear increases risk of other corneal infections (Pseudomonas) but dendritic pattern indicates HSV. Reduced acuity signals corneal involvement requiring urgent ophthalmology care.

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY:
HSV-1 reactivation in trigeminal ganglion infects corneal epithelium; epithelial disease may progress to stromal keratitis and scarring with vision loss. Topical corticosteroids on epithelial HSV can enlarge ulcer, prolong infection, and increase stromal involvement risk.

DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH:
Fluorescein exam showing dendritic ulcer; consider HSV PCR/culture if atypical. Differentiate bacterial keratitis (contact lens, dense infiltrate, purulent discharge), acanthamoeba (severe pain out of proportion), and UV keratitis. Red flags: hypopyon, large infiltrate, scleritis—urgent ophthalmology.

MANAGEMENT:
Oral valacyclovir or topical antivirals (trifluridine, ganciclovir gel per local availability) for epithelial keratitis; ophthalmology co-management for follow-up and stromal disease. Avoid topical steroids unless ophthalmologist treats concomitant stromal inflammation with antiviral cover. Discontinue contact lenses until healed; educate on recurrence. Bacterial conjunctivitis therapy alone is ineffective and delays appropriate antiviral treatment.

KEY POINTS:
Dendritic corneal ulcer = HSV keratitis—antiviral therapy, ophthalmology referral, no steroids. Contact lens wear requires cessation during active keratitis. Topical antibiotics alone miss viral etiology.
bulletExplanation:
- CORRECT: B (Antiviral therapy, ophthalmology referral, stop contacts, avoid steroids).
- Dendritic fluorescein uptake on cornea indicates herpes simplex keratitis.
- Treat with oral/topical antivirals; corticosteroids can worsen HSV corneal disease.
- Antibiotic conjunctivitis treatment or continued lens wear is inappropriate.
